planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, fertile to average soil in full sun; West Indian mahogany is one of the few native mahogany trees in North America, naturally occurring in tropical hardwood hammocks of extreme south Florida and the Keys.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun in zone 10–12; West Indian mahogany is one of the most prized timber trees in the world — the original 'true mahogany' of the Caribbean cabinet-making tradition, producing close-grained, durable, reddish-brown wood used in fine furniture, musical instruments, and boat building since the 16th century. It is the state tree of the Dominican Republic. In south Florida and the Keys, it grows naturally in tropical hardwood hammock and is the only native mahogany in North America. Widely planted as a street and shade tree in Miami, it develops into a stately, large, semi-deciduous tree with a dense, spreading canopy. The large, hanging seed capsules split into five valves to release winged seeds. Critically endangered in its natural range due to centuries of timber harvesting; planting supports conservation of this culturally important species.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; chinaberry is naturalizing throughout the US Gulf South and is considered invasive in many areas. Avoid planting near natural areas — it spreads prolifically by bird-dispersed seeds. The berries are toxic to humans and most mammals.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun, with awareness of invasive potential; chinaberry is an Asian deciduous tree widely naturalized across the US Gulf South, Caribbean, and tropics worldwide. The fragrant, lilac-purple flower clusters in spring are highly ornamental and have given the tree the names 'Persian lilac' and 'pride of India.' The clusters of small, yellow berries that persist through winter are striking ornamentally but are toxic to humans, dogs, and livestock (though consumed by birds, which distribute the seeds widely). The tree is drought and heat tolerant, fast-growing, and adaptable to almost any well-drained soil — characteristics that, combined with prolific bird-dispersed seeding, make it invasive in many parts of the South. Used in agroforestry in tropical countries for natural insecticide compounds (azadirachtin relatives from the same Meliaceae family as neem). Caution: do not plant near natural areas.
sources: USDA Forest Service Invasive Plants List

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; Florida boxwood grows naturally in the understory of tropical and subtropical hardwood hammocks of extreme south Florida and the Keys, tolerating the dense shade of the hammock canopy.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, moderately fertile soil in partial to full shade; Florida boxwood is a native evergreen shrub of the tropical hardwood hammock understory in south Florida and the Keys, bearing small, shiny, leathery leaves that superficially resemble true boxwood (Buxus), hence the common name. Though unrelated to northern boxwood, it serves a similar function as a shade-tolerant, small-leaved, evergreen understory shrub that can be sheared into hedges and formal shapes. Small orange-red to red fruits provide food for tropical birds. One of the few native south Florida shrubs genuinely adapted to deep shade within the hammock canopy, making it valuable for shaded landscape settings in zone 10. An important but little-known native for ecologically appropriate south Florida landscaping.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; crabwood is a characteristic tree of tropical hardwood hammocks in south Florida and the Keys, growing naturally beneath the main hammock canopy as an understory to mid-canopy species.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin, rocky or sandy, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; crabwood is a native evergreen tree of south Florida tropical hardwood hammocks, typically growing as an understory to mid-canopy species in the diverse, dense hammock forest. The small, dark green, elliptical leaves are glossy and leathery, and the plant produces dense, attractive foliage year-round. The catkin-like flower spikes are inconspicuous. Small, black fruits are eaten by white-crowned pigeons, the indicator bird of the south Florida hammock ecosystem. The wood is dense and hard, used historically for tool handles and small crafts. Crabwood is an important structural species of the Miami-Dade hammock ecosystem — a habitat that has been reduced by over 90% from original extent. Suitable for naturalistic south Florida landscaping under and around larger hammock trees.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after removing the fleshy seed coat in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; soap tree grows naturally in coastal hammocks and scrub of south Florida and the Caribbean and is drought and salt tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; soap tree is a small evergreen to semi-evergreen tree of the Caribbean and south Florida with pinnately compound leaves and clusters of small white flowers followed by translucent, fleshy, amber fruits containing high concentrations of saponins — natural soap compounds. The crushed or moistened fruits lather readily in water and have been used throughout the Caribbean for centuries as soap for washing clothes and hair. Unlike the more common western soapberry (S. drummondii) of the southern plains, soap tree is a subtropical to tropical species better adapted to the hot, humid conditions of south Florida. The fruits are also used in some ayurvedic traditions and in natural cleaning products. A drought-tolerant, low-maintenance native tree providing both ornamental interest and functional soap production for eco-conscious gardeners in zone 9–11.
